question.text: In which derivative segment does the hedger need to take an opposite position in futures to the expected price movement of the underlying asset due to an inverse price-yield relationship?

answer.text: Interest rate derivatives — because bond prices and interest rates move inversely
Currency derivatives — because exchange rates and interest rates move inversely
Equity derivatives — because stock prices and dividends move inversely
Commodity derivatives — because commodity prices and storage costs move inversely

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In interest rate derivatives, if a bond holder expects interest rates to rise, bond prices will fall. To hedge, the holder sells bond futures (goes short on price/long on interest rate). This inverse relationship between bond prices and yields is unique to fixed income. **The Trap:** Currency and equity derivatives have a direct relationship between the underlying and the derivative — if the asset price goes up, the futures price also goes up. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV Ch5 discusses this inverse relationship extensively. Series I and VIII have direct price relationships for hedging. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always remember: In bond futures, selling futures = going long on interest rates. This reversal confuses many candidates.
